agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation policy for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle events: placement, invoicing, and shipping.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of placement, invoicing, and shipping."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so that it will not be fulfilled or shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated for an order that locks in pricing and represents an irreversible financial commitment."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created and enters the system."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The act of dispatching goods to the customer, after which the order is considered fulfilled."},{"term":"Invoicing at Placement","definition":"Operations policy whereby an invoice is generated at the exact moment an order is placed, to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product policy that could be revised by leadership. It defines a cancellation window, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request should succeed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares invoicing irreversible. Allowing cancellation of an invoiced order would create accounting inconsistencies — revenue recognized, tax implications, ledger corruption. This is a financial integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, a cancellation request must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. A VP could change this to invoice-at-shipment or invoice-at-pick without corrupting the system.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice record must exist immediately after placement completes."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — BR-1 says this should succeed (not yet shipped), but BR-2 + BR-3 together mean an invoice already exists at placement, so cancellation must be rejected."},{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — If invoicing is synchronous with placement and fails, is the order in a cancellable state? BR-3 assumes invoicing always succeeds at placement."},{"scenario":"Credit memo or refund as alternative to cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Finance may allow reversing the economic effect of an invoice via credit memo without 'cancelling' the invoice itself. This could satisfy all three rules but is not mentioned in requirements."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If the contradiction is resolved by preventing all cancellations (favoring Finance/Ops), customers lose flexibility and refund requests will increase through support channels. If resolved by allowing cancellation of invoiced orders, financial ledger integrity is at risk and audit/compliance issues may arise.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any resolution that decouples invoicing from placement introduces pricing risk and requires a new mechanism to guarantee price locks.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the system silently rejects all cancellations due to the invoicing constraint, customer satisfaction and trust will degrade significantly.","risk":"Implementing any one stakeholder's rule without resolving the contradiction guarantees violating another stakeholder's constraint. Shipping code with this ambiguity risks financial compliance violations or a broken cancellation UX.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order post-placement if Finance/Ops rules dominate, contradicting the product promise of pre-shipment cancellation."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If PM's rule dominates, invoiced orders get cancelled, breaking ledger integrity and creating audit exposure."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is moved away from placement to resolve the conflict, they lose the pricing lock mechanism they depend on."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of confused customers whose cancellation requests are rejected despite a stated policy of pre-shipment cancellation."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Cancelling irreversible invoices may have tax and revenue recognition implications under ASC 606 or equivalent standards."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The three requirements form a logical contradiction. BR-3 (invoice at placement) + BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) = orders can never be cancelled after placement. BR-1 (cancel any time before shipment) directly contradicts this conclusion. No amount of analysis can resolve this without a stakeholder decision. Confidence is low because no valid acceptance criteria can be defined until the contradiction is resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation","Invoicing irreversibility is a genuine financial integrity constraint","The three rules as stated are mutually incompatible"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's rule should take precedence","Whether a credit memo / reversal mechanism is an acceptable resolution","Whether 'invoiced' has a narrower meaning than assumed (e.g., draft vs. finalized invoice)"],"assumptions":["Invoice generation at placement is synchronous and always succeeds","'Invoiced' means a finalized, ledger-posted invoice — not a draft or pro-forma","There is no existing credit memo or invoice reversal process in the system"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. Operations invoices at placement (BR-3). Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2). Therefore no order can ever be cancelled after placement. But Product says orders can be cancelled any time before shipment (BR-1). These three statements cannot all be true simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Finance yields — introduce a credit memo or invoice reversal mechanism so that invoiced orders CAN be economically reversed without violating ledger integrity. Cancellation triggers a credit memo rather than deleting the invoice.","B: Operations yields — move invoicing to a later lifecycle stage (e.g., at pick, at pack, or at shipment) so there is a cancellation window between placement and invoicing. Pricing lock is achieved through a separate mechanism (e.g., price snapshot on order).","C: Product yields — change cancellation policy to state orders cannot be cancelled once placed, only before order confirmation or within a very short grace period before invoicing completes.","D: Hybrid — invoice at placement as a 'draft/hold', finalize invoice at shipment. Cancellation allowed while invoice is in draft state. Finance defines 'invoiced' as finalized only."],"recommendation":"Option D (Hybrid) or Option A (Credit Memo) are the most business-friendly resolutions. Option D preserves all three stakeholders' intent by distinguishing draft vs. finalized invoices. Option A is standard practice in most ERP systems — invoices are never deleted, but their economic effect is reversed via credit memos. Recommend convening Product, Finance, and Operations to choose. Do NOT proceed with implementation until resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are logically contradictory and cannot be implemented as a coherent system.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is incompatible with the conjunction of BR-2 and BR-3. Implementing all three simultaneously is impossible."}]}
